coredata.dc:description: As a novel chalcogenide photocatalyst, MnPS3 suffered from limited visible light absorption, high photogenerated electron-hole recombination, and low hole oxidation capability due to its high valence band (VB) potential. In this work, the novel MnPS3 nanosheets-Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) composites were fabricated by immobilizing NCDs with terminal amine groups on Na+ intercalated MnPS3 nanosheets for a greatly enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. MnPS3 nanosheets of 400 nm with Mn2+ vacancies are produced in high yield by NaCl intercalation and subsequent exfoliation in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP). NCDs with 5 nm are evenly loaded on the surface of MnPS3 nanosheets of 400 nm via strong chemical interactions of ammonium sulfate salts formed at the interface. The MnPS3-NCDs composites exhibit enhanced light absorption at 500–600 nm, reduced charge recombination and notably promoted photocatalytic activity in relative to neat MnPS3 nanosheets. MnPS3-NCDs composite with the NCDs content of 16.5% possessed the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 339.63 μmol·g−1·h−1 with good cycling stability, which is 9.17 times that of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. The type-II MnPS3-NCDs heterojunction is conducive to the efficient interfacial carrier transport and the significantly improved photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity. Our work confirmed that the non-toxic MnPS3 could possess photocatalytic performance comparable to CdS, which will be promising to become an attractive visible-light driven photocatalyst in environmental purification and energy applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a novel chalcogenide photocatalyst, MnPS3 suffered from limited visible light absorption, high photogenerated electron-hole recombination, and low hole oxidation capability due to its high valence band (VB) potential. In this work, the novel MnPS3 nanosheets-Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) composites were fabricated by immobilizing NCDs with terminal amine groups on Na+ intercalated MnPS3 nanosheets for a greatly enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. MnPS3 nanosheets of 400 nm with Mn2+ vacancies are produced in high yield by NaCl intercalation and subsequent exfoliation in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP). NCDs with 5 nm are evenly loaded on the surface of MnPS3 nanosheets of 400 nm via strong chemical interactions of ammonium sulfate salts formed at the interface. The MnPS3-NCDs composites exhibit enhanced light absorption at 500–600 nm, reduced charge recombination and notably promoted photocatalytic activity in relative to neat MnPS3 nanosheets. MnPS3-NCDs composite with the NCDs content of 16.5% possessed the highest photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate of 339.63 μmol·g−1·h−1 with good cycling stability, which is 9.17 times that of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. The type-II MnPS3-NCDs heterojunction is conducive to the efficient interfacial carrier transport and the significantly improved photocatalytic hydrogen generation activity. Our work confirmed that the non-toxic MnPS3 could possess photocatalytic performance comparable to CdS, which will be promising to become an attractive visible-light driven photocatalyst in environmental purification and energy applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the exhaustion of fossil energy and the aggravation of ecological pollution, it is particularly valuable and urgent to discover alternative new energy. As one of the most promising alternative fuels, hydrogen has attracted widespread attention because of its high-energy-density and environment friendliness [1–3]. The ideal and sustainable process is utilizing high-quality nano-semiconductor electrocatalysis and photocatalysis to split water into hydrogen [4–7]. Therefore, the development of novel stable photocatalyst with high efficiency is one of the key technologies to improve the photocatalytic hydrogen production capability. Since 1972, many types of semiconductor nanomaterials such as TiO2, CdS and g-C3N4 have been utilized in hydrogen generatation under photocatalytic process [8–11]. Unfortunately, however, the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity of single semiconductor is usually inhibited due to the narrow light absorption range, poor long-term stability, photocorrosion, and high charge recombination. The fabrication of type-Ⅱ, Z-scheme and S-scheme heterojunctions by compounding different semiconductors into composites can effectively suppress the photo-generated carrier recombination to improve the hydrogen evolution performance [12]. However, the low solar-to-hydrogen efficiency below 1% hindered its further industrial expectations [13]. It is still quite necessary to develop novel semiconductor materials with significantly promoted photocatalytic actives.

As a widely concerned chalcogenide material, CdS has attracted significant attentions in the photocatalytic H2 evolution because of its fit band gap with good visible light response and appropriate conduction band level for the reduction of H+ from water [14–16]. However, CdS is highly toxic and susceptible to photocorrosion. It is urgent to develop nontoxic stable chalcogenide materials with good visible light absorption and suitable band structure for photocatalytic hydrogen production. MnPS3 has the similar band-gap of 2.39 eV to CdS with more negative conduction band (CB) potential (−1.58 eV) than the H+/H2 redox potential [17], which is expected as a promising alternative material to CdS in photocatalytic applications. Moreover, MnPS3 is nontoxic with excellent stability, and features layered two dimensional structures, which is convenient to be peeled off into two dimensional (2D) nanosheets with plenty of exposed active surface sites [18,19]. However, due to limited surface area, low efficient visible light response and fast photo-generated carrier recombination, neat MnPS3 nanosheets exhibited very poor photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. The reported photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of MnPS3 nanosheets prepared from the chemical vapor deposition is only 3.1 µmol·h−1·g−1 [20]. In our earlier work, the exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets in N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) present a slightly higher photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 21.2 µmol·h−1·g−1 [18]. The exfoliation process was inefficient with low yield under long time sonication treatment with the protection of inert gas. After the combination with Cs4W11O35 nanoparticles, the photocatalytic H2 production rate of 99.61 μmol·g−1·h−1 can be achieved for MnPS3-Cs4W11O35 composites with optimized mass ratio [18]. The development of more efficient exfoliation methods and the construction of novel heterojunctions to boost the photocatalytic hydrogen production activity are of great significance for the potential applications of MnPS3-based photocatalytic materials.

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) are a new class of granular carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes less than 10 nm. It is composed of a graphitized SP2 hybrid core and different functional groups such as carbon-oxygen, amine and carboxylic acid groups [21]. It has sparked extensive research interest for its fascinating physical and chemical properties, such as low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, high specific surface area, excellent visible light absorption, and unique upconversion fluorescence properties [22–24]. Due to the presence of terminal functional groups, carbon dots can be easily combined with other semiconductors such as TiO2, CdS, WO3 and g-C3N4 through strong interfacial chemical interactions [15,25–28]. Due to the excellent light absorption and strong interface chemical interaction, significantly promoted photocatalytic activities can be achieved by the construction of carbon dots based heterojunction [29,30]. It is highly desired that the combination of N-doped carbon dots and MnPS3 nanosheets can apparently improve its photocatalytic activities through extended light absorption and efficient interfacial charge transport. Furthermore, the immobilization of carbon dots on MnPS3 nanosheets could prevent the aggregation of MnPS3 nanosheets and favor the dispersion of MnPS3 nanosheets in water, which significantly affected the photocatalytic H2 generation performance. However, so far, there are no reports on nitrogen-doped carbon dots modified MnPS3 composite photocatalysts.

In this paper, nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) and exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets were compounded from the mixed dispersion to obtain MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions with outstanding photocatalytic hydrogen production performance. The morphology, microstructure, crystal structure, optical properties and interface chemical interactions of MnPS3-NCDs composites were thoroughly explored through various testing methods. The novel intercalation-exfoliation approach improved the exfoliation efficiency and the yield of MnPS3 nanosheets, effectively introduces Mn2+ vacancies, and facilitates to better compounding with carbon dots. Thereafter, the significantly enhanced H2 production rate of 339.63 μmol·g−1·h−1 is achieved by MnPS3-NCDs composites with optimized mass ratio, which is nearly ten times of neat MnPS3 nanosheets. Our work provided a novel highly active chalcogenide photocatalyst with wide application potential.

Laminated phosphorus trisulfide (MnPS3, 99 %) powders are produced by chemical vapor deposition approach from Sixcarbon Technology in Shenzhen. Anhydrous citric acid (C6H8O7, 99%), urea (CH4N2O, 99%), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP, 99%), 3-ethoxypropylamine (C5H13NO, 98%), sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98%) and sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 98%) were purchased from TCI Shanghai company. Chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6, 99%) was received from Shanghai Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. All the chemicals are used as original status without further purification.

MnPS3 nanosheets were prepared by a liquid exfoliation process according to our former work with some modification [18]. Typically, 200 mg of bulk MnPS3 powder and 1 g of NaCl were blended with 30 mL of distilled water in a round bottom flask, and the mixture was suffered from an ion intercalation process at 70 ℃ under the protection of argon for 24 h. After centrifuging the intercalated green suspension, and the resulting Na+ intercalated MnPS3 precipitation was dispersed in 200 mL of N-methylpyrrolidone with 2 mL 3-ethoxypropylamine, which was purged with argon for 20 min. The mixture was kept stirring for a day after 8 h of sonication at 250 W, and a green uniform suspension was generated. After removal of unexfoliated MnPS3 by a low speed centrifugation procedure, MnPS3 nanosheets were acquired through a high-speed centrifugal separation process at 8,500 rpm from the remaining clear dispersion, which were additionally purified several times by deionized (DI) water to remove residual NaCl (Scheme 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and MnPS3/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were prepared by a hydrothermal method from urea and citric acid according to the literatures [31]. MnPS3-NCDs composites were prepared by a liquid phase compounding process. Specifically, 5–15 mL of 15 mg·mL−1 NCDs aqueous stock solution was blended with into 50 mL NMP with 1 mg·mL−1 MnPS3 dispersion, which was additionally stirred for two days to anchor NCDs on MnPS3 nanosheets. The final MnPS3-NCDs composites were purified by centrifugation twice with ethanol and DI water. The samples with different NCDs volume of 0, 10, 15, and 20 mL were named as MnPS3-NCDs, MnPS3-NCDs-10, MnPS3-NCDs-15, and MnPS3-NCDs-20 for further photocatalytic application.

Hitachi HT7700 transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Thermo Talos F200S field emission TEM are applied for the morphology observation. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of MnPS3 were measured by Bruker Dimension FastScan spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded from Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer with Cu Kα irradiation. Raman spectra measurements were performed by the confocal Labram HR800 Raman spectrometer. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were obtained from Themor-Filsher Nicolet 6700 spectrometer. Elemental mapping were carried out by an EDX detector resourced with a 200 kV TEM-2100F. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data, valence band XPS (VB-XPS) spectra and the Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) result were collected by a Thermo Fisher Esca-lab 250 XI spectrometer with a monochromatic Al-Ka source. UV–vis spectroscopy (UV–vis-DRS) spectra and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of photocatalysts were determined by using a Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus spectrophotometer and a Horiba Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer, respectively. An Auto-lab PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation was used for the photoelectrochemical performance.

The photocatalytic experiments were implemented by a GEL-SPH2N photochemical reactor with cooling water system from Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology Company. 50 mg photocatalysts and 5 mg of H2PtCl6 were dispersed into 100 mL DI water with 5 mmol each of Na2S·9H2O and Na2SO3 as sacrificial agents. The photocatalyst suspensions were vacuumed to completely remove air before illuminated by a 300 W xenon lamp. The generated H2 amount was recorded per hour by a GC-7920 gas chromatography.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of MnPS3/NCDs composites
ce:section.ce:para: Because MnPS3 is prone to be oxidized under the hydrothermal process, a simple exfoliation approach was employed to produce 2D MnPS3 nanosheets in NMP, which efficiently assisted the exfoliation of layered chalcogenides [32–34] and some other two-dimensional materials [35,36]. With the assisted intercalation of NaCl, thin MnPS3 monosheets are obtained with higher exfoliation efficiency. Under continuous stirring under 70℃, some Mn2+ in MnPS3 will be replaced by Na+ to generate Mn2+ vacancies in the expanded MnPS3 powder by Na+ intercalation, which is similar to the K+ and Li+ intercalation of MnPS3 [37–39] (Fig. S1). As a result, Na+ intercalated MnPS3 deposition will be more prone to be exfoliated into MnPS3 nanosheets with high yield in the solvents of NMP and 2% 3-ethoxy propylamine [33].

AFM is applied to investigate the morphology and thickness of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. As presented in Fig. 1 a, typical 2D nanosheet morphology with the size of about 400 nm can be observed for MnPS3 nanosheets assisted with Na+ intercalation. Moreover, the thickness of MnPS3 nanosheets from the two-line scans is about 5.6 nm, which is the same as MnPS3 nanosheets exfoliated in NMP without Na+ intercalation [17]. AFM results indicate that Na+ intercalation can greatly increase the yield of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets with no evident changes in the lateral size and thickness. Fig. 1b shows the XRD patterns of started MnPS3, NaCl refluxed MnPS3 and exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. Bulk MnPS3 possesses typical diffraction peaks of monoclinic MnPS3 (ICDD, PDF#78–0495). After refluxing in NaCl solution, the (001) peaks of the NaCl refluxed MnPS3 slightly shifted from 13.8° to 13.6°, suggesting a larger layer spacing of 6.51 Å than 6.42 Å of bulk MnPS3. This indicates that bulk MnPS3 powders are not thoroughly intercalated by Na+ to create NaxMn1-xPS3 as KxMn1-xPS3 by the similar intercalation process [37–39], which may be caused by the larger ionic radius of Na+. However, the slightly increased layer spacing by refluxing in NaCl saturated solution indicates the partial intercalation of MnPS3 by sodium ions, which will facilitate the next-step exfoliation process in NMP. MnPS3 nanosheets under the exfoliation in NMP with and without NaCl refluxing process exhibit the same interlayer distance of 17.6 Å, which is significantly enlarged relative to 6.42 Å of the starting bulk MnPS3. Moreover, the crystal lattice spacing of {110} facets in the HRTEM images of bulk MnPS3 and exfoliated MnPS3 sheets are 0.30 and 0.47 nm (Fig.S2), respectively, further validating its efficient exfoliation. Raman spectroscopy is utilized to detect the differences between bulk MnPS3 and exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the clear Raman band centered at around 275 cm−1 for MnPS3 nanosheets is associated to the E1g vibration modes of the SPS and SPP bonds, and the A1g peak at and 384 cm−1 is resulted from the combined symmetric stretching of PP and PS bonds in PS3 groups [40]. A slight shift to a lower Raman shift of about 8 cm−1 for the A1g and E1g bands of MnPS3 nanosheets occurs relative to that of bulk MnPS3, which is due to the filling of Mn2+ vacancies by NMP solvent and 3-ethoxypropylamine.

The morphology of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets, NCDs and MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions were investigated by the TEM observation. The as-synthesized MnPS3 nanosheets possess a thin layered structure with a size of about 400 nm similar to that observed by AFM (Fig. 2
a). As shown in Fig. 2b, NCDs are well dispersed with the uniform size of about 5 nm. Fig. 2c and d presents TEM images of MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite with the NCDs volume of 15 mL. It is clearly seen that NCDs are anchored uniformly on the surface of MnPS3 nanosheets without obvious morphology and size changes. In addition, TEM images of MnPS3-NCDs composites with different NCDs contents in Fig. S3 can also verify the perfect combination of MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs. The clear lattice fringes at the HRTEM image in Fig. 2e can be witnessed with lattice spacings of 0.47 and 0.30 nm, which are ascribed to the (110) and (201) crystal planes of MnPS3 nanosheets [20]. Simultaneously, a neat array of lattice stripes with 0.32 nm spacing for MnPS3 nanosheets is attributed to the (002) facet of sp2 hybridized carbon from NCDs [41]. TEM observations validate that the binding with NCDs effectively suppressed the aggregation of MnPS3 nanosheets.

The elemental components were obtained from the Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) detectors equipped with TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 2i, C, N, O, Mn, P and S elements are detected in the sample of MnPS3-NCDs composites. From the EDS elemental mapping images in Fig. 2g, h, j, k, and I, all the C, N, O, Mn, P and S elements are evenly distributed. The NCDs weight percentages in the final MnPS3-NCDs composites calculated from the EDX analyses are 10.3%, 16.5% and 20.1% for the samples of MnPS3-NCDs-10, MnPS3-NCDs-15 and MnPS3-NCDs-20, respectively (Fig. S4). Uniform distribution and good adhesion of 2D-0D MnPS3-NCDs composites would be beneficial for higher photocatalytic performance.

After the combination with NCDs, Raman spectrum of MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite in Fig. 3a presents the Raman scattering peaks at 376.3, 267.5 and 236.4 cm−1 from the stretching vibration of PS3 groups in MnPS3. The clear two characteristic Raman peaks at around 1370 and 1587 cm−1 were assigned to D band and G band of graphitic carbon structures in NCDs [42]. Moreover, the bands at 616 and 980 cm−1 correspond to OSO bond bending vibration of SO4 2− and the symmetric stretching vibration of SO bond, respectively, confirming the existence of sulfate radical for the formation of ammonium salt. The Raman analysis further validated the successful anchoring of NCDs on MnPS3 nanosheets.

The crystal structures of exfoliated MnPS3 nansheets and MnPS3-NCDs are investigated by X-ray diffractometer. In Fig. 3b, MnPS3-NCDs retains a strong diffraction peak of (001) crystal plane at 8.5° with a slight left-deviation of about 5° relative to the bulk MnPS3. After loading carbon dots, some weak new diffraction peaks can be observed at 2θ = 20°∼30° in the XRD patterns of MnPS3-NCDs composites, which may be caused by the formation of ammonium salt though the chemical interaction between NCDs and MnPS3 nanosheets. At the magnified picture in Fig. 3c, the clear weak peaks at 20.4°, 22.8°, 29.2°, 29.7° and 65.6° for MnPS3-NCDs composites are assigned to the diffraction peaks of (NH4)2SO4 (PDF#76-0579), confirming the strong chemical interaction between MnPS3 and NCDs. Moreover, the peaks of the ammonium salt are more obvious with the increase of the volume content of carbon dots. Therefore, we believe that the terminal –NH2 groups of carbon dots interact with MnPS3 to form ammonium sulfate at the interface of MnPS3-NCDs composites during the compounding process. However, there are no obvious characteristic peaks of NCDs in the XRD patterns of MnPS3-NCDs composites because of its low content as well as the peak overlap with {002} facets of MnPS3 at similar diffraction angle.

The chemical bonding and their chemical interactions in the synthesized samples are characterized by FTIR spectroscopy, as presented in Fig. 3d. For neat NCDs, clear vibration bands at 1670, 1400 and 1245 cm−1 are resulted from the CO, CO and COC bonds, respectively [43,44]. Besides, a strong diffraction peak from NH bond at 3210 cm−1 confirms the successful nitrogen doping of carbon dots [45]. The abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing groups on the surface of NCDs will be conducive to its uniform dispersion and perfect compounding with MnPS3 nanosheets. For the FTIR spectra of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15 composites, the two strong vibration bands centered at 609 and 557 cm−1 are ascribed to the cleavage of PS3 asymmetric stretching vibration peak at 570 cm−1 of neat MnPS3, which is caused by the distortion of PS bonds in the M2P2S6 crystals [46]. It is generally believed that bulk MnPS3 were successfully exfoliated with the rupture of the PS bond. Furthermore, the occurrence of this splitting peaks indicates the existence of Mn2+ ion vacancy in the main MnPS3 layer, which further confirms the intercalation of Na+ into MnPS3 and the successful exfoliation of MnPS3 [47]. The weak diffraction band at 450 cm−1 was connected with the PP vibrations in MnPS3 nanosheets [48]. Moreover, in comparison to MnPS3 nanosheets, the two new vibration bands at 3158 and 1400 cm−1 for MnPS3-NCDs composites are attributed to organic ammonium ions and CO bonds, indicating the strong chemical interactions between MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs [49].

The XPS analyses are implemented to further validate the strong chemical interaction in MnPS3-NCDs composites. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 4 a reveals that the existence of Mn, P, and S elements are originated from MnPS3 nanosheets, while the presence of C, O and N elements are derived from NCDs. This confirms the successful preparation of MnPS3-NCDs composites. From high resolution Mn 2p spectra of neat MnPS3 nanosheets and MnPS3-NCDs-15 in Fig. 4b, the two independent peaks at 652.9 and 641.1 eV corresponding to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 from Mn2+ can be observed without evident change after the combination with NCDs. However, the shoulder peak at 645.1 eV belonging to partially oxidized MnO2 shifted to a higher binding energy of 645.8 eV upon binding to NCDs, possibly due to the interaction with NCDs. The high resolution P 2p spectra for neat MnPS3 nanosheets and MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite are given in Fig. 4c. The P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2 peaks at 131.7 and 132.5 eV are assigned to PS bonding in starting MnPS3 nanosheets. Compared with MnPS3 nanosheets, the P 2p levels of MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite are shifted towards higher values by 0.4 eV to 132.1 and 132.9 eV, and an additional peak appeared at 134.9 eV is referred to oxidation state of P4O10 [50,51]. The two deconvoluted characteristic peaks at 162.3 and 163.6 eV for MnPS3-NCDs-15 are ascribed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 from polysulfide (Fig. 4d), which shifted about 0.5 eV towards higher values relative to MnPS3 nanosheets [52]. Moreover, in comparison with MnPS3 nanosheets, both P 2p and S 2p levels for the MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite shift about 0.5 eV to a higher level, which is caused by the intense chemical interaction of S-(NH4)-O bonds [53]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. 4e can be divided into three peaks at 288.3, 286.2 and 284.8 eV, which are attributed to the binding energies of CO/CN, CO/CN and CC, respectively [54]. For MnPS3-NCDs composite, the two major N 1s peaks at 401.2 and 400.3 eV in Fig. 4f are attributed to nitrogen atoms with NH/ON-C bond and (C)3-N bond, further confirming the effective compounding of MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs [49]. The XPS analyses corroborate the strong interaction and good combination of MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs, which is in well accordance with our above mentioned TEM, XRD, FT-IR and Raman characterization results.

Due to the strong interaction of NCDs and MnPS3 nanosheets, it is expected that its phocatalytic activity can be evidently improved. The photocatalytic H2 evolution activity of MnPS3-NCDs composites are assessed in the sacrificial solution of Na2S·9H2O and Na2SO3. All the MnPS3-based photocatalysts of 50 mg are dispersed in water and irradiated with light for 6 h to totally reduce chloroplatinic acid. As given in Fig. 5 a and 5b, the results show that the H2 production rate of 37.56 μmol·g−1·h−1 by MnPS3 nanosheets with Na+ intercalation is 1.77 times that of 21.22 μmol·g−1·h−1 by MnPS3 without Na+ intercalation [17]. The Na+ intercalation boosted the yield of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets with introduced Mn2+ vacancies, thereby significantly enhancing the photocatalytic activity. However, the photocatalytic H2 generation activity of MnPS3 nanosheets with Na+ intercalation remains quite low due to the rapid charge recombination of photogenerated carrier during photocatalytic H2 evolution process [17]. After compounding with the different volume content of carbon dots, the photocatalytic H2 generation performance is boosted significantly (Fig. 5a). With the increased amount of carbon dots, the photocatalytic H2 production activities of MnPS3-NCDs composites are evidently improved as a volcano data curve in Fig. 5b. The best photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 339.63 μmol·g−1·h−1 is acquired by MnPS3-NCDs composite with 15 mL of NCDs dispersion, which was 9.18 times that of MnPS3 nanosheets. With the sacrificial agent of methanol (Fig. 5c), the H2 evolution rate of MnPS3-NCDs-15 dropped down significantly because methanol is more difficult to be oxidized by the holes of MnPS3 than Na2S·9H2O and Na2SO3.

The photocatalytic stability of MnPS3-NCDs composites was further inspected by 4 cycling experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 5d, the significantly boosted photocatalytic H2 generation rate for MnPS3-NCDs-15 composites and MnPS3 nanosheets can be achieved in the second cycle after the complete reduction of H2PtCl6. No evident changes take place for the hydrogen evolution activity of MnPS3-NCDs-15 composites and MnPS3 during another three additional cycling experiments. Moreover, after photocatalytic H2 evolution experiments, the morphology and the crystalline structures of MnPS3-NCDs-15 keep unchanged (Fig. S5), demonstrating the outstanding stability of MnPS3-NCDs composites.

Table.1 summarized the photocatalytic H2 generation data by the relative materials from the literatures, such as CdS nanosheets[9], CdS quantum dots [10], CdS nanoparticle [14], MnPS3/Eosin Y [18], MnPS3/Carbon Fiber[20], C-dots/CdS [15,27], MnPS3/Cs4W11O35 [17], and C-dots/g-C3N4 [28]. It is excited to find that our modified MnPS3-NCDs composites exhibit even higher photocatalytic performance than CdS-based photocatalysts. This suggests that MnPS3 can completely replace the highly toxic and easily photocorroded cadmium-based photocatalytic materials. The photocatalytic results validate that the Na+ intercalation and further compounding with NCDs can greatly boost H2 production rate of MnPS3 without variation in stability.

Since the light absorption has important influence on the photocatalytic performance, pristine MnPS3 nanosheets and MnPS3-NCDs composites are characterized by UV–vis diffraction spectra. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the absorption edge of pure MnPS3 nanosheets is at 496 nm. After loading NCDs on MnPS3 nanosheets, the absorption edges of MnPS3-NCDs-10, MnPS3-NCDs-15 and MnPS3-NCDs-20 are at about 446, 442 and 445 nm, respectively, which possess a slight blue shift relative to MnPS3 nanosheets. Furthermore, MnPS3-NCDs composites exhibit higher visible light absorption intensity in the 400–700 nm range. Based on UV–vis absorption data, the optical band gap (Eg) can be obtained according to the formula: αhν = A (hν-Eg) n
/2 [55]. The Eg values of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15 composite in Fig. 6a are about 2.56 and 2.64 eV. The valence band XPS spectra were further used to measure the valence bands of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15. The maximum valence bands (VBM) of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15 are +0.76 and +0.73 eV in Fig. 6b, corresponding to their Fermi levels (Ef) for p-type semiconductor. The Mott-Schottky plots of MnPS3 in Fig. 6c possess negative slopes for typical p-type semiconductors, which is consistent with previous literature [56]. Generally speaking, for n-type and p-type semiconductors, Efs are usually close to ECB and EVB with 0.1–0.2 eV in-band shift [57]. The EVB values of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15 are about +0.98 and +1.05 eV according to Fig. 6c and MnPS3-NCDs-15 in Fig. S6. As calculated from the formula of EG = ECB-EVB [58], the specific position of valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) of MnPS3 can be determined as +0.98 and −1.58 eV, respectively. Since the VB value is close to the Ef for p-type semiconductors [59], the approximate Ef potential can be determined by Mott-Schottky and VB-XPS plots. Therefore, the calculated Efs of MnPS3 and MnPS3-NCDs-15 are 0.22 and 0.32 eV. From the Mott-Schottky curve of NCDs in Fig. 6d and the Ultraviolet Photoelectron Spectroscopy (UPS) result of spin-coated NCDs film in Fig. 6e, the calculated VB and CB values of NCDs are −1.26 and +3.34 eV , respectively, which are in good accordance with the results reported by the previous literature [63].

Photoluminescence (PL) measurement for MnPS3 nanosheets and MnPS3-NCDs was implemented to reflect the charge recombination state of semiconductors. Fig. 6f presents the PL spectra of MnPS3-based photocatalysts excited at 250 nm. For all the MnPS3-based samples, the clear emission band at 400 nm is ascribed to the 3d orbital transitions of Mn2+ [60]. With the increased NCDs contents, the PL intensities of MnPS3 nanosheets are gradually quenched, suggesting the effective charge migration in MnPS3-NCDs composites, which will result into lower charge recombination rate and better electron-hole separation efficiency.

Electrochemical analyses were performed to explore the light response and charge transfer of MnPS3 based composites. As given in Fig. 7 a, MnPS3-NCDs composites possessed higher transient photocurrent response than neat MnPS3 nanosheets because of more effective carrier migration and boosted electron-hole separation [61,62], which is in good accordance with PL analyses. EIS results of all the photocatalysts are displayed as semicircle curves in Fig. 7b. The smaller impedance arc radius is observed for MnPS3-NCDs-15 composites, indicating the reduced electronic resistance (RCT) and more effective migration of carriers.

Based on energy band structure analyses and characterizations, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for the promoted photocatalytic activity of MnPS3-NCDs composites is concluded in Fig. 8 . According to the above results, the VB and CB positions for neat MnPS3 nanosheets are at +0.98 and −1.58 eV, and the VB and CB values of NCDs are −1.26 and +3.34 eV. For the n-type NCDs, the Ef is about 0.2 eV lower than its CB position. Therefore, the calculated Ef of −1.06 eV for NCDs is higher than +0.78 eV of MnPS3 nanosheets, the electrons could transfer from NCDs to MnPS3 nanosheets until the equilibrium state with equal Efs, which induced upward and downward bending of energy bands of NCD and MnPS3 nanosheets, respectively. Consequently, more positive charges are left on NCDs, while more negative charges are gathered on MnPS3 nanosheets. Under solar light irradiation, both MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs are excited simultaneously, and photo-generated electrons are migrated from VB to CB, respectively. The CB electrons of MnPS3 nanosheets will be prone to move to the CB of NCDs, and the VB holes of NCDs will be migrated to the VB of MnPS3 nanosheets driven by internal electric field, which is facilitated by the interfacial electrostatic interaction through the generation of S-(NH4)-O bonds. The photogenerated electrons collected on the CB of NCDs will reduce H2O into hydrogen, and the holes left on the VB of MnPS3 nanosheets will oxidize the sacrifices of Na2SO3 and Na2S·9H2O. The strong interfacial interaction as well as the introduction of Mn2+ vacancies greatly enhanced the light absorption and the interfacial charge transfer in the MnPS3-NCDs composites. The creation of type-II MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions boosted the charge separation, greatly prolonged the lifetime of photo-generated electrons on the CB of MnPS3 nanosheets, and significantly enhanced the photocatalytic H2 evolution activity.

We fabricated the novel type-Ⅱ MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions with significantly improved photocatalytic H2 evolution activity by compounding the Na+ intercalated MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs. The exfoliated Na+ intercalated MnPS3 nanosheets present a transverse size of 400 nm and a thickness of about 5.5 nm with significantly expanded interlayer distance of 17.6 Å. The intercalation of NaCl leads to a more effective exfoliation of MnPS3 nanosheets and the introduction of Mn2+ vacancies. NCDs of 5 nm were uniformly anchored on MnPS3 nanosheets in the MnPS3-NCDs composites. The strong chemical interaction between MnPS3 nanosheets and NCDs take place by forming ammonium sulfate salt at the interface, which leads to a higher photocatalytic H2 evolution activity than earlier reported MnPS3-based and CdS-based composites [9, 10, 15, and 18]. The highest H2 evolution rate for MnPS3-NCDs composites with the NCDs weight percent of 16.5 % reaches 339.63 μmol·g−1·h−1, which is 9.17 times that of exfoliated MnPS3 nanosheets. The significantly enhanced photocatalytic H2 evolution activity is resulted from the enhanced visible light absorption, the promoted charge transfer, and the inhibited charge recombination by type-II MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions with strong interface chemical interaction. Future work will be concentrated in introducing different types of vacancies to additionally boost the photocatalytic performance of MnPS3. The Na+ intercalation and the construction of MnPS3-NCDs heterojunctions play important roles in developing novel non-toxic chalcogenide photocatalyts with the promoted water splitting performance.
